title: Superellipse
content:
A superellipse, also known as a Lamé curve after Gabriel Lamé, is a closed curve resembling the ellipse, retaining the geometric features of semi-major axis and semi-minor axis, and symmetry about them, but a different overall shape. In the Cartesian coordinate system, the set of all points ("x", "y") on the curve satisfy the equation where "n", "a" and "b" are positive numbers, and the vertical bars around a number indicate the absolute value of the number. Specific cases.

This formula defines a closed curve contained in the rectangle −"a" ≤ "x" ≤ +"a" and −"b" ≤ "y" ≤ +"b". The parameters "a" and "b" are called the "semi-diameters" of the curve. If "n" < 2, the figure is also called a hypoellipse; if "n" 2, a hyperellipse.

When "n" ≥ 1 and "a" = "b", the superellipse is the boundary of a <a href="ball%20%28mathematics%29" ball</a of R2 in the <a href="norm%20%28mathematics%29%23p-norm" "n"-norm</a. The extreme points of the superellipse are (±"a", 0) and (0, ±"b"), and its four "corners" are (±"sa, ±sb"), where formula_2 (sometimes called the "superness"). Mathematical properties.

When "n" is a positive <a href="rational%20number" rational number</a "p"/"q" (in lowest terms), then each quadrant of the superellipse is a <a href="algebraic%20curve" plane algebraic curve</a of order "pq". In particular, when "a" = "b" = 1 and "n" is an even integer, then it is a <a href="Fermat%20curve" Fermat curve</a of degree "n".

In that case it is non-singular, but in general it will be <a href="Singular%20point%20of%20an%20algebraic%20variety" singular</a. If the numerator is not even, then the curve is pieced together from portions of the same algebraic curve in different orientations. The curve is given by the <a href="parametric%20equation" parametric equation</a s (with parameter formula_3 having no elementary geometric interpretation) where each ± can be chosen separately so that each value of formula_3 gives four points on the curve.
